chunks.text: \chapter{Printing Files} \label{printing files} \setcounter{mpfootnote}{0} \setcounter{footnote}{0} \renewcommand{\thempfootnote}{\arabic{mpfootnote}} {\it The information in this section is also available on-line if you have an account on an Instru

ilable on-line if you have an account on an Instructional machine. See the file /usr/pub/printer.help for the most current information. } The two major families of UNIX (System V and BSD) have different sets of printer commands. The HPs run ``HP-UX

ent sets of printer commands. The HPs run ``HP-UX'' which is a System V based system; The DECs run ``Ultrix'' which is a BSD based system. If you aren't sure what type of system you're on, type {\bf uname}.\index{uname} \section{How to Print from {

uname}.\index{uname} \section{How to Print from {\em HP-UX} Systems} \index{file!printing}\index{printing} The printing commands used by HP-UX are {\bf lp}, {\bf cancel}, and {\bf lpstat}. \subsection{ {\bf lp} }\index{lp@{\bf lp} command} Th

ction{ {\bf lp} }\index{lp@{\bf lp} command} The {\bf lp} command is used to submit a print job to the print queue. You need to specify which printer you want to send the job to by using the `-dprintername' command line option. (Unless you h

-dprintername' command line option. (Unless you have selected a default printer -- see Section \ref{defprint}.) \begin{quote} {\tt \%}~~{\bf lp}~~{\bf -d}{\it lw274} {\it filename} \\ {\tt request id is lw274-87 (1 file)} \end{quote} For more in

id is lw274-87 (1 file)} \end{quote} For more information, type {\bf man lp}. \subsection{ {\bf cancel} }\index{cancel@{\bf cancel} command} The {\bf cancel} command is used to remove a print job from the print queue. You must specify either

from the print queue. You must specify either the request id or printer name on the command line. \begin{quote} {\tt \%}~~{\bf cancel}~~{\it job\_number} \end{quote} where {\it job\_number} is obtained from the first column of {\bf lpstat} out

obtained from the first column of {\bf lpstat} output. For example, \begin{quote} {\tt \%}~~{\bf cancel lw274-87} \\ {\tt request ``lw274-87'' cancelled} \end{quote} You can also place the printer name on the command line to cancel the curr

nter name on the command line to cancel the currently printing job. \begin{quote} {\tt \%}~~{\bf cancel lw274} \\ {\tt request ``lw274-88'' cancelled} \end{quote} If you use the {\bf -a} option with the printer name, you will cancel ALL of

ion with the printer name, you will cancel ALL of your print jobs in that printer's queue. \begin{quote} {\tt \%}~~{\bf cancel lw274~~-a} \\ {\tt request ``lw274-89'' cancelled \\ request ``lw274-90'' cancelled \\ request ``lw274-91'' cancell

74-90'' cancelled \\ request ``lw274-91'' cancelled } \end{quote} For more information, type {\bf man cancel}. \subsection{ {\bf lpstat} }\index{lpstat@{\bf lpstat} command} The {\bf lpstat} command is used to list ALL of your print jobs in th

and is used to list ALL of your print jobs in the queues of ALL printers; if it returns nothing, then all of your requests have either printed or been cancelled. \begin{quote} {\tt \%}~~{\bf lpstat} \end{quote} With the {\bf -a} option,

lpstat} \end{quote} With the {\bf -a} option, it will report to you the list of available printers. \begin{quote} {\tt \%}~~{\bf lpstat~~-a} \\ {\tt lw347 accepting requests since Jan 5 14:08 \\ lw274 accepting requests since Dec 14 18:07 \

\\ lw274 accepting requests since Dec 14 18:07 \\ lw275 accepting requests since Dec 14 18:07 } \end{quote} If you wish to see the print jobs other users have queued, use the {\bf -o} option. \begin{quote} {\tt \%}~~{\bf lpstat -o}~~$|$~~{\

\begin{quote} {\tt \%}~~{\bf lpstat -o}~~$|$~~{\bf more} \end{quote} This may be a rather long listing; if you are only interested in seeing the queue for one particular printer, place the printer name after the {\bf -o} option. Please not

ter name after the {\bf -o} option. Please note the spacing (or lack of) is important. \begin{quote} {\tt \%}~~{\bf lpstat}~~{\bf -olw347}~~$|$~~{\bf more} \end{quote} For more information, type {\bf man lpstat}. \section {How to Print from

pe {\bf man lpstat}. \section {How to Print from {\em DEC ULTRIX} systems} \index{file!printing}\index{printing} \subsection{ {\bf lpr} } \noindent The {\bf lpr} command\index{lpr@{\bf lpr} command} places one or more files in a print queue for pri

places one or more files in a print queue for printing. A print queue is where files wait before being printed on a particular printer. The {\bf lpr} command has the following form: \begin{quote} {\tt \%}~~{\bf lpr}~{\bf -P}{\it printer\_name}~~{\

\tt \%}~~{\bf lpr}~{\bf -P}{\it printer\_name}~~{\it filename} \end{quote} where {\it printer\_name} is the name associated with a printer queue and {\it filename} is the name of the file that you would like to print. (You can specify more than one

ld like to print. (You can specify more than one filename on this command by putting a blank between the filenames.) If you omit the {\bf -P} option, your file will not be printed unless you have a default printer set. See Section \ref{defprint}.

efault printer set. See Section \ref{defprint}. Some of the more commonly used options are: \begin{center} \def\arraystretch{1.5} \begin{tabular}{ll} \hline Option & Function \\ \hline {\tt-}{\bf P}{\em printername} &Selects which printer to us

f P}{\em printername} &Selects which printer to use ({\bf required}) \\ {\tt-}{\bf T}{\em title} &Prints the title at the head of each page\\ {\tt-}{\bf m} &Sends you mail when the print job is completed\\ {\tt-}{\bf Ol} &Print th

nt job is completed\\ {\tt-}{\bf Ol} &Print the file with a ``landscape'' orientation\\ {\tt-}{\bf g} &Specifies that the file is data generated by the ``plot'' routine \\ \hline \end{tabular} \end{center} For more information, type {\bf ma

} \end{center} For more information, type {\bf man lpr}. \subsection{ {\bf lpq} } To display a list of jobs waiting to print on a particular printer queue, enter:\index{lpq@{\bf lpq} command} \begin{quote} {\tt \%}~~{\bf lpq -P}{\it printer\_name} \
quote} {\tt \%}~~{\bf lpq -P}{\it printer\_name} \end{quote} For example, \begin{quote} {\tt \%}~~{\bf lpq -Plw105} \\ {\tt lw105 is ready and printing via dev} \\ {\tt Rank Owner Job Files Total
Owner Job Files Total Size} \\ {\tt active c123-aa 430 homework.1 136392 bytes} \\ \end{quote} For more information, type {\bf man lpq}. \subsection{ {\bf lprm} } \noindent To delete one of

bsection{ {\bf lprm} } \noindent To delete one of your own print jobs from a printer queue do the following:\index{lprm@{\bf lprm} command} \begin{quote} {\tt \%}~~{\bf lprm -P}{\it printer\_name}~~{\it job\_number} \end{quote} where {\it job\_numbe

\it job\_number} \end{quote} where {\it job\_number} is obtained from the third column of {\bf lpq} output. For example, \begin{quote} {\tt \%}~~{\bf lprm -Plw105 430} \end{quote} You can use `~-~' instead of a job\_number to remove ALL of your pri

instead of a job\_number to remove ALL of your print jobs in that printer's queue. For example, \begin{quote} {\tt \%}~~{\bf lprm -Plw121 ~-~} \\ {\tt pasteur.EECS.Berkeley.EDU: dfA540po.EECS.Berkeley.EDU dequeued \\ pasteur.EECS.Berkel

rkeley.EDU dequeued \\ pasteur.EECS.Berkeley.EDU: cfA540po.EECS.Berkeley.EDU dequeued \\ pasteur.EECS.Berkeley.EDU: dfA541po.EECS.Berkeley.EDU dequeued \\ pasteur.EECS.Berkeley.EDU: cfA541po.EECS.Berkeley.EDU dequeued } \end{q

.EDU: cfA541po.EECS.Berkeley.EDU dequeued } \end{quote} \section{ {\bf enscript} } \index{enscript@{\bf enscript} command} Enscript is another useful printing command that allows more options than lpr or lp. The default behavior of enscript is to

pr or lp. The default behavior of enscript is to print the filename, date, and page number at the top of each page of output. \noindent To print with enscript, use the command: \begin{quote} {\bf enscript [}{\it options\/}{\bf] [}{\it filename\/}{\b

cript [}{\it options\/}{\bf] [}{\it filename\/}{\bf]} \end{quote} Some of the more commonly used options are: \begin{center} \def\arraystretch{1.5} \begin{tabular}{ll} \hline Option & Function \\ \hline {\tt-}{\bf P}{\em printername} &Selects whi

ine {\tt-}{\bf P}{\em printername} &Selects which printer to use ({\bf required on DECS}) \\ {\tt-}{\bf d}{\em printername} &Selects which printer to use ({\bf required on HPS}) \\ {\tt-}{\bf 2} &Sets two column mode\\ {\tt-}{\bf

2} &Sets two column mode\\ {\tt-}{\bf r} &Rotates output 90 degrees on page (landscape mode) \\ {\tt-}{\bf 2r} &Does both of the above (recommended) \\ \hline \end{tabular} \end{center} For more information, type {\bf man enscript}.

r} For more information, type {\bf man enscript}. \section{Default Printer} \label{defprint} You can set a default printer so that you do not need to always specify the printer name when you print (with the `{\bf -d}' or `{\bf -P}' option). On the

with the `{\bf -d}' or `{\bf -P}' option). On the HPs, use the LPDEST variable; on the DECs, use PRINTER. For example, to send all your print jobs to the laserwriter in 274 Soda, type: \begin{quote} {\tt \%}~~{\bf setenv LPDEST lw274} \index{LPDEST

{\tt \%}~~{\bf setenv LPDEST lw274} \index{LPDEST variable} ~~~OR \\ {\tt \%}~~{\bf setenv PRINTER lw274} \index{PRINTER variable} \\ \end{quote} This is not always convenient, however. You may want to change the printer you're using if you freque

t to change the printer you're using if you frequently work in different laboratory rooms, or the printer you like to use is down. Therefore, it is best to always specify the printer name whenever you print. \section{Available Printers} \index{prin

u print. \section{Available Printers} \index{printers} Most EECS Instructional lab rooms have a printer. Current printer information is displayed in the laboratories and on-line in /usr/pub/EECS.Facilities.
